title: Gradient Descent
text: Gradient descent is an optimization algorithm that minimizes a loss function by iteratively moving parameters in the direction of the negative gradient. The step size is controlled by the learning rate. Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) estimates the gradient from a small random batch of examples rather than the full dataset, which makes each update cheaper and adds noise that can help escape poor local minima.

title: Learning Rate
text: The learning rate is a hyperparameter that scales the size of each parameter update during training. If it is too high, training can diverge or oscillate; if it is too low, training is slow and may get stuck. Learning rate schedules, such as warmup followed by cosine decay, adjust the rate over time to improve convergence.

title: Overfitting
text: Overfitting occurs when a model learns patterns specific to the training data, including noise, and therefore fails to generalize to unseen data. It is characterized by low training error but high validation error. Common remedies include collecting more data, regularization, early stopping, and reducing model capacity.

title: Regularization
text: Regularization discourages overly complex models to reduce overfitting. L2 regularization (weight decay) penalizes the squared magnitude of weights and shrinks them toward zero. L1 regularization penalizes the absolute magnitude of weights and tends to produce sparse solutions where many weights become exactly zero.

title: Dropout
text: Dropout is a regularization technique that randomly sets a fraction of neuron activations to zero during each training step. This prevents the network from relying too heavily on any single neuron and forces it to learn redundant, robust representations. At inference time dropout is disabled and activations are scaled accordingly.

title: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
text: The bias-variance tradeoff describes the tension between two sources of error. High bias means the model is too simple and underfits the data. High variance means the model is too sensitive to the training set and overfits. Increasing model complexity typically lowers bias but raises variance, so the goal is to balance the two to minimize total error.

title: Train, Validation, and Test Splits
text: Datasets are commonly split into three parts. The training set is used to fit model parameters. The validation set is used to tune hyperparameters and select models without touching the test set. The test set is held out until the very end to give an unbiased estimate of generalization performance.

title: Cross-Entropy Loss
text: Cross-entropy loss measures the difference between a predicted probability distribution and the true distribution, and is the standard loss for classification. For a single example it is the negative log probability the model assigns to the correct class. Minimizing cross-entropy pushes the predicted probability of the correct class toward one.

title: Backpropagation
text: Backpropagation is the algorithm used to compute gradients of the loss with respect to every parameter in a neural network. It applies the chain rule of calculus, propagating error signals backward from the output layer to the input layer. These gradients are then consumed by an optimizer such as gradient descent to update the weights.

title: Batch Normalization
text: Batch normalization normalizes the inputs to a layer using the mean and variance computed over the current mini-batch. This stabilizes and accelerates training by reducing internal covariate shift, and it allows higher learning rates. It also has a mild regularizing effect because the batch statistics introduce noise.

title: Attention and Transformers
text: The attention mechanism lets a model weigh the relevance of different input tokens when producing each output, computing a weighted sum of value vectors based on query-key similarity. The Transformer architecture is built almost entirely from self-attention and feed-forward layers, and unlike recurrent networks it processes all tokens in parallel, which makes it highly scalable.

title: Embeddings
text: An embedding is a dense vector representation of a discrete item such as a word, token, or document, learned so that semantically similar items lie close together in vector space. Distances or cosine similarities between embeddings can be used for retrieval, clustering, and as inputs to downstream models.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) improves a language model's answers by first retrieving relevant documents from an external knowledge source and then conditioning generation on those documents. This grounds responses in up-to-date or domain-specific information and reduces hallucination, without requiring the model to be retrained.
